neighborhoods.name: Downtown Tulsa
primary_groups: White oil executives, merchants, professionals, speculators
neighborhoods.character: Downtown Tulsa was the command center of the Mid-Continent oil field — the place where deals were made, leases were traded, and fortunes were created and destroyed in single afternoons. After the Glenn Pool strike of 1905, oil company headquarters rose along Boston Avenue and Main Street. The art deco skyscrapers that still define Tulsa's skyline — the Philtower, the Philcade, the Boston Avenue Methodist Church — were built with oil money in the 1920s. The wealth was extraordinary and concentrated: Tulsa had more millionaires per capita than any city in America. The poverty was in the oil fields, the refineries, and across the railroad tracks in Greenwood.
key_institutions: Tulsa Chamber of Commerce (the 'Oil Capital' boosters)
Boston Avenue Methodist Church (art deco landmark)
Tulsa World and Tulsa Tribune newspapers
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Tulsa around {year}, the downtown skyline was rising with oil money — art deco towers built on petroleum, every one of them a monument to the fact that someone had guessed right about where the oil was.

neighborhoods.name: Greenwood District (Black Wall Street)
primary_groups: African American residents — professionals, merchants, laborers, families
neighborhoods.character: The Greenwood District, north of the Frisco railroad tracks, was the most prosperous Black community in America. By 1921, Greenwood had over 600 Black-owned businesses: hotels, restaurants, theaters, law offices, medical practices, a hospital, grocery stores, furriers, and a bus system. O.W. Gurley, a wealthy Black landowner, had purchased 40 acres and sold lots exclusively to Black buyers, creating the foundation for a self-sustaining economy. A dollar circulated within Greenwood an estimated 36 times before leaving the community. The Dreamland Theatre showed first-run films. The Stradford Hotel was one of the largest Black-owned hotels in the country. Greenwood Avenue was called 'Black Wall Street' because the concentration of Black wealth had no parallel anywhere in the nation. On May 31-June 1, 1921, a white mob — deputized and armed by city officials, some arriving in private aircraft — destroyed all of it. Over 300 Black residents were killed. Thirty-five blocks were burned to the ground. Over 10,000 Black residents were left homeless. It was the single worst act of racial violence in American history.
key_institutions: Vernon AME Church (only standing original Greenwood structure)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} lived in Greenwood before June 1921, {pronoun_subject} was part of something that existed almost nowhere else — a Black community so prosperous that white Tulsa could not tolerate it. What the mob destroyed in those two days was not just property but proof that Black people could build anything white people could, and that was exactly what made it a target.

neighborhoods.name: Maple Ridge
primary_groups: Wealthy white oil families, executives, and professionals
neighborhoods.character: Maple Ridge was where the oil money lived. Grand Tudor, Colonial Revival, and Mediterranean-style mansions lined wide boulevards south of downtown. The Skelly, Sinclair, and Phillips families built estates here. The neighborhood was Tulsa's statement that it was not a frontier boomtown but a proper American city with proper American wealth. Country clubs, private schools, and garden parties defined the social calendar. The wealth was new — most of it less than twenty years old — and displayed with the confidence of people who had made it themselves and intended everyone to know it.
key_institutions: Philbrook Museum (later — originally the Waite Phillips mansion)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Maple Ridge was built with oil money — if {ancestor_name} worked for one of the oil families, the difference between the mansion and the worker's cottage told the whole story of who got rich in the oil boom and who did the work.

neighborhoods.name: Red Fork / West Tulsa
primary_groups: White working-class oil field and refinery workers, some Native American residents
neighborhoods.character: Red Fork was where the oil was actually found — the 1901 Sue Bland No. 1 well at Red Fork was the first commercial oil strike in Indian Territory. The neighborhood became a refinery district: tank farms, pipe yards, and processing facilities lined the Arkansas River bottoms. Working-class families lived in frame houses and company housing near the refineries, breathing petroleum fumes and industrial smoke. The work paid well but the living conditions were rough — flooding from the Arkansas River was a recurring hazard, and the air quality near the refineries was poor. West Tulsa was the other side of the oil economy from Maple Ridge.
key_institutions: West Tulsa industrial corridor along Arkansas River
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Red Fork was where the oil came out of the ground and where the men who pulled it out lived — if {ancestor_name} worked the refinery district in West Tulsa, the paycheck was good and the air was not.

major_employers.name: Oil Companies (Skelly, Sinclair, Phillips Petroleum, Cities Service)
major_employers.wages: Roughnecks and roustabouts earned $3.00-6.00/day (1910s-1920s) — premium wages for dangerous work. Refinery workers earned $2.50-4.50/day. Skilled positions like tool dressers and drillers earned $5.00-8.00/day. Office and executive positions paid substantially more. Oil field wages attracted workers from across the region.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Mid-Continent oil field was one of the most productive petroleum regions in the world from 1905 through the 1930s. Drilling was dangerous — blowouts, fires, falling pipe, and hydrogen sulfide gas killed and maimed workers regularly. Refinery work involved exposure to benzene, petroleum fumes, and extreme heat. The industry was racially stratified: white workers held the skilled and semi-skilled positions, Black workers were confined to the most dangerous and lowest-paid labor, and Native allottees whose land sat atop oil deposits were subjected to systematic fraud through the guardian system.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Tulsa's oil fields paid better than almost anything a working man could find — if {ancestor_name} came to Tulsa for oil work, the wages were real, but so was the danger: a roughneck's life expectancy was not something the recruiters mentioned.

major_employers.name: Refineries and Pipe Manufacturing
major_employers.wages: Refinery workers earned $2.50-5.00/day (1910s-1920s). Skilled trades (pipe fitters, boilermakers) earned $4.00-7.00/day. The refining sector provided steadier employment than drilling, which was boom-and-bust.
major_employers.working_conditions: Tulsa's refineries processed crude oil from the Glenn Pool, Cushing, and Osage fields into gasoline, kerosene, and lubricants. The Mid-Continent supply chain generated a manufacturing sector: pipe companies, tank builders, and oil field supply firms clustered along the Arkansas River and the railroad lines. The work was industrial — hot, dirty, and hazardous — but it offered year-round employment and wages that farming could not match.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The refineries ran around the clock — if {ancestor_name} worked the stills in West Tulsa, the shift work meant regular pay while the wildcatters were either making fortunes or going broke.

major_employers.name: Greenwood District Small Businesses (Pre-1921)
major_employers.wages: Highly variable. Greenwood's Black professionals — doctors, lawyers, dentists — earned comfortable incomes. Merchants' earnings depended on the volume of trade within the segregated economy. O.W. Gurley, J.B. Stradford, and A.J. Smitherman were among the wealthiest Black businesspeople in America.
major_employers.working_conditions: Greenwood's economy was self-contained by necessity and by design. Segregation forced Black residents to patronize Black-owned businesses, which created a closed-loop economy of remarkable vitality. A dollar spent in Greenwood stayed in Greenwood — circulating through the grocer, the barber, the doctor, the church collection plate, and back to the grocer. This economic density created wealth that was visible and threatening to white Tulsa. Every successful Black business on Greenwood Avenue was a refutation of the racial hierarchy that justified segregation.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} owned a business on Greenwood Avenue before 1921, {pronoun_subject} was building something that the rest of America said Black people could not build. The white mob that burned it knew exactly what it was destroying.

major_employers.name: Railroad (Midland Valley, Frisco, Katy Lines)
major_employers.wages: Section hands earned $1.50-2.50/day (1910s). Skilled shop workers earned $3.00-5.00/day. The Frisco tracks through Tulsa formed the racial dividing line — Greenwood was north of the tracks, white Tulsa was south.
major_employers.working_conditions: Multiple railroad lines converged in Tulsa, making it a transportation hub for the oil industry. The Frisco (St. Louis-San Francisco Railway) tracks ran east-west through the city and served as the de facto racial boundary — Black residents lived north of the tracks in Greenwood, white residents south. This geographic division, created by railroad infrastructure, became the line that the white mob crossed on May 31, 1921.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The Frisco tracks divided Tulsa into two cities — if {ancestor_name} was Black, home was north of the tracks in Greenwood; if white, south. On the night of May 31, 1921, the mob crossed those tracks heading north.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Tulsa grew from a Creek Nation cattle shipping point to a city of 72,000 in two decades. Early housing was rough — tents, shacks, and boarding houses near the oil fields and railroad. As oil wealth accumulated, the housing stock bifurcated sharply: mansions in Maple Ridge and substantial homes in midtown for the wealthy, modest frame cottages and shotgun houses for oil field workers and laborers. In Greenwood, the housing ranged from substantial brick commercial buildings on Greenwood Avenue to well-built frame residences on residential streets — a prosperous Black neighborhood by any standard.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Racial segregation was absolute and enforced. Black residents were confined to the Greenwood District north of the Frisco railroad tracks. White working-class neighborhoods clustered near the refineries and industrial areas in West Tulsa and Red Fork. Wealthy white families concentrated in Maple Ridge and later in south Tulsa. The few Native American residents in Tulsa were in an ambiguous position — Creek allottees whose land the city was built on, increasingly marginalized as the non-Native population exploded. After the 1921 massacre, city officials attempted to rezone Greenwood for industrial use to prevent rebuilding — the attempt failed, and Black residents rebuilt, but the intent was clear.
housing_patterns.trajectory: For white workers: boarding house (weeks to months) to rented cottage (1-3 years) to owned frame house (3-7 years). Oil field wages accelerated the timeline. For Black residents in Greenwood: similar trajectory through the 1910s, building toward substantial homeownership — then the 1921 massacre reset everything to zero. Greenwood residents rebuilt, but the accumulated wealth of fifteen years was destroyed in two days. After 1921, the trajectory restarted from rubble.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Tulsa's racial violence reached a level that has few parallels in American history. The 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre was the systematic destruction of the most prosperous Black community in the nation by a white mob that included deputized civilians and, by some accounts, private aircraft dropping incendiary devices. The massacre was followed by decades of official silence, cover-up, and denial. The KKK was a dominant political force in 1920s Tulsa and Oklahoma.
specific_incidents: 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre (May 31-June 1): White mob attacked the Greenwood District after a disputed encounter between Dick Rowland (a Black teenager) and Sarah Page (a white elevator operator). Over 300 Black residents killed, 35 blocks burned, over 10,000 left homeless, approximately 6,000 Black residents detained in internment camps. Property damage estimated at $1.8 million (1921 dollars). No white perpetrator was ever convicted. Mass graves remained unexamined for nearly a century.

Post-massacre land grab: White business interests attempted to rezone the destroyed Greenwood District for industrial and railroad use, preventing Black residents from rebuilding on their own land. The attempt was defeated by Black legal action led by attorney B.C. Franklin.
KKK dominance of Tulsa politics throughout the 1920s — cross burnings, intimidation of Black residents, Catholics, Jews, and labor organizers
nativist_organizations: Ku Klux Klan (controlled Tulsa and Oklahoma politics in the 1920s)
Tulsa city officials who facilitated the 1921 massacre through deputization of white mob members

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Mt. Zion Baptist Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Pre-1921 records were destroyed in the massacre. Post-rebuilding membership rolls and sacramental records available. The church's destruction and rebuilding is itself a primary document of the massacre.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Mt. Zion Baptist was brand new in 1921 — the congregation had just finished paying for the building when the mob burned it to the ground. If {ancestor_name} was a member, the church's story IS the story: destroyed and rebuilt, like Greenwood itself.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Vernon AME Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Vernon AME is the only standing structure from the original Greenwood District that survived the 1921 massacre. Church records from the early 1900s may survive. The building itself is a landmark — the basement served as a refuge during the massacre.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Vernon AME is the only original building left standing from Black Wall Street — if {ancestor_name} worshiped here, the church walls are the last physical connection to what Greenwood was before the massacre took everything else.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Greenwood Cultural Center
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community — preserving the history of Greenwood and the 1921 massacre
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Archives of Greenwood history, oral histories, photographs, and massacre documentation. Research library available to genealogists and historians.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Greenwood Cultural Center holds what survived and what was remembered — if {ancestor_name} lived in Greenwood, the center's archives and oral history collections may be the only place where their story is preserved.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Booker T. Washington High School
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American students in Tulsa's segregated school system
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Student enrollment records, yearbooks, and alumni records. The school survived the 1921 massacre (it was located slightly outside the main burn zone) and served as a community anchor during rebuilding.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Booker T. Washington High School was where Greenwood educated its children — if {ancestor_name} or their children attended, the school records document the generation that rebuilt after 1921, or the generation before that built something worth destroying.

source_references.author: Scott Ellsworth
source_references.title: Death in a Promised Land: The Tulsa Race Riot of 1921
source_references.notes: The first serious scholarly study of the 1921 massacre, breaking decades of official silence. Ellsworth's research, based on oral histories and archival sources, established the factual foundation for all subsequent scholarship on the massacre.

source_references.author: Hannibal B. Johnson
source_references.title: Black Wall Street: From Riot to Renaissance in Tulsa's Historic Greenwood District
source_references.notes: Documents the rise, destruction, and rebuilding of the Greenwood District. Johnson provides detailed descriptions of pre-massacre businesses, institutions, and community life, drawing on oral histories and community records.

source_references.author: Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot
source_references.title: Tulsa Race Riot: A Report by the Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921
source_references.notes: Official state commission report documenting the massacre, assessing damages, and recommending reparations. The report confirmed the scale of destruction, identified mass grave sites, and established the state's responsibility for facilitating the violence.

source_references.author: Tim Madigan
source_references.title: The Burning: Massacre, Destruction, and the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921
source_references.notes: Narrative history of the massacre drawing on survivor testimony and archival research. Madigan traces the events of May 31-June 1, 1921, in detail and documents the subsequent cover-up and the survivors' long fight for recognition.
